content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Unit-Test-Driven Prompting for Improved LLM Code Generation",
  "problem": "Current LLMs often generate code that fails to satisfy functional requirements, particularly when prompts lack explicit specification or feedback. Standard prompting methods do not leverage executable feedback loops to refine generations in-context.",
  "motivation": "Improving functional correctness in code generation is crucial for real-world adoption of LLM-based programming assistants. Recent advances show that incorporating feedback or test signals can enhance performance, but practical, prompt-based methods remain underexplored.",
  "method": "We propose a dynamic prompting approach where, for each task, the prompt includes a function signature and a minimal unit test harness (e.g., assert statement). The LLM is asked to generate code, which is then executed in a sandbox. If the output fails the unit test, the LLM receives the error message and stack trace as additional context in a follow-up prompt, encouraging iterative refinement. This loop is repeated up to a fixed number of attempts or until the generated code passes the test.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate on HumanEval and MBPP datasets. Baseline: standard zero-shot and few-shot code generation prompts. Metrics: pass@1 and pass@k (functional correctness after k attempts), as well as the number of iterations to success. This tests whether prompt-embedded feedback can measurably improve generation quality versus static prompting."
}
